1:
"And if his oblation be a sacrifice of peace offering, if he offer it of the herd; whether it be a male or female, he shall offer it without blemish before the LORD." The witnesses note the peace offering of the herd, male or female, without blemish. Gill notes the Targums render it the sacrifice of holy things, or of thanksgivings; the peace offering of the herd might be male or female, but without blemish. The witnesses note God appointing the peace offering of the herd to be male or female, without blemish, offered before the LORD; the peace offering, the sacrifice of fellowship and thanksgiving, freely accepted — that the offering of peace may be male or female, but unblemished: "if his oblation be a sacrifice of peace offering, if he offer it of the herd; whether it be a male or female, he shall offer it without blemish"; the witnesses note the kind, "a sacrifice of peace offering" — rendered by the Targums "the sacrifice of holy things" or "of thanksgivings," the offering of peace, fellowship, and thankful praise, in which the offerer shared and feasted before God; the witnesses note the latitude, "whether it be a male or female" — that, unlike the burnt offering (male only), the peace offering might be of either sex, a greater freedom in this offering of communion; the witnesses note the requirement, "without blemish before the LORD" — that, though either sex, it must be perfect and unblemished, a type of Christ the Lamb without blemish; the witnesses note the type the commentators draw, that the peace offering shadows Christ our peace (Eph. 2:14), by whom we have reconciliation and communion with God, and the believer's fellowship and thanksgiving; the witnesses note that in Christ God meets his people in peace; so that the peace offering is male or female, unblemished; he shall offer it without blemish before the LORD. The witnesses note And if his oblation be a sacrifice of peace offering, if he offer it of the herd; whether it be a male or female. The witnesses note he shall offer it without blemish before the LORD.

The witnesses note the peace offering of the herd, male or female (a greater freedom than the burnt offering's male only), but without blemish — the sacrifice of fellowship and thanksgiving, in which the offerer shared and feasted before God; a type of Christ our peace, by whom we have reconciliation and communion with God, the believer's fellowship and thanksgiving, in Christ God meeting his people in peace.

So the witnesses make the verse the peace offering, the sacrifice of fellowship, freely accepted. The lesson the expositors draw is that the peace offering, male or female but unblemished, was the offering of communion and thanksgiving, a type of Christ our peace, by whom we have fellowship with God. The witnesses note the peace offering: "a sacrifice of peace offering" — Gill notes of thanksgivings; the peace offering; the of thanksgivings; the of holy things; the fellowship; the feasted. The expositors observe the male or female: "male or female" — a greater freedom; the male or female; the a greater freedom; the unlike the burnt offering; the either sex; the communion. The witnesses press the without blemish: "without blemish" — perfect; the the Lamb. The expositors draw the lesson on the Christ our peace: a type of Christ our peace; the Christ our peace; the Christ; the our peace; the reconciliation; the communion. The expositors note the meets in peace: in Christ God meets his people in peace. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the he shall offer it without blemish before the LORD, and the peace offering. The verse thus records the peace offering — teaching that it was male or female but unblemished, the sacrifice of fellowship, a type of Christ our peace.
2:
"And he shall lay his hand upon the head of his offering, and kill it at the door of the tabernacle of the congregation: and Aaron's sons the priests shall sprinkle the blood upon the altar round about." The witnesses note the offerer laying his hand on the head of the peace offering, killing it, and the priests sprinkling its blood. Gill notes he laid his hand, his right hand with strength, the Targum of Jonathan says, on the head of the offering, killed it at the door, and the priests sprinkled the blood round about the altar. The witnesses note the offerer laying his hand on the head of the peace offering, killing it at the door, and the priests sprinkling the blood round the altar; the laying on of hands and the sprinkled blood, the same atoning ground for the peace offering — that the peace offering too rests on the substitute and the blood: "he shall lay his hand upon the head of his offering, and kill it at the door... and... the priests shall sprinkle the blood upon the altar round about"; the witnesses note the laying on, "lay his hand upon the head" — the offerer identifying himself with the victim, as in the burnt offering; the witnesses note the slaying, "kill it at the door of the tabernacle" — the victim put to death in the appointed place; the witnesses note the blood, "the priests shall sprinkle the blood upon the altar round about" — the blood applied for atonement; the witnesses note that our fellowship and communion with God are grounded on the atoning death of Christ; the witnesses note that peace is made by the blood of his cross (Col. 1:20); so that the peace offering too rests on the substitute and the sprinkled blood; the priests shall sprinkle the blood upon the altar round about. The witnesses note he shall lay his hand upon the head of his offering, and kill it at the door of the tabernacle of the congregation. The witnesses note and Aaron's sons the priests shall sprinkle the blood upon the altar round about.

The witnesses note the offerer laying his hand on the head of the peace offering, killing it, and the priests sprinkling its blood — the same atoning ground for the peace offering as for the burnt; even the offering of fellowship resting on atonement, for there is no peace with God but by the blood of the substitute; our communion with God grounded on the atoning death of Christ, peace made by the blood of his cross.

So the witnesses make the verse the laying on of hands and the sprinkled blood for the peace offering. The lesson the expositors draw is that the peace offering too rested on the substitute and the blood, our fellowship grounded on atonement, peace made by the blood of the cross. The witnesses note the lay his hand: "lay his hand upon the head" — Gill notes identifying; the lay his hand; the identifying; the with the victim; the as the burnt offering; the the head. The expositors observe the kill it: "kill it at the door" — put to death; the slain. The witnesses press the sprinkle the blood: "sprinkle the blood... round about" — for atonement; the applied. The expositors draw the lesson on the rests on atonement: even fellowship rests on atonement; the rests on atonement; the even fellowship; the rests; the atonement; the no peace. The expositors note the the cross: peace made by the blood of his cross. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and Aaron's sons the priests shall sprinkle the blood upon the altar round about, and the atonement. The verse thus records the hands and the blood — teaching that the peace offering too rested on the substitute and the blood, our fellowship grounded on atonement.
3:
"And he shall offer of the sacrifice of the peace offering an offering made by fire unto the LORD; the fat that covereth the inwards, and all the fat that is upon the inwards," The witnesses note the fat covering the inwards offered by fire to the LORD. Gill notes the priest offered, not all of it, but some of it, even the fat that covereth the inwards and all the fat upon the inwards, an offering made by fire to the LORD. The witnesses note the priest offering by fire the fat that covered the inwards and all the fat upon them; the giving of the fat, the best and richest, to God, while the rest was shared — that the fat is God's portion in the peace offering: "he shall offer of the sacrifice of the peace offering an offering made by fire... the fat that covereth the inwards, and all the fat that is upon the inwards"; the witnesses note that not all the victim, but a part, was burnt to God, the rest being shared (the breast and shoulder to the priests, the rest to the offerer for the feast); the witnesses note the part for God, "the fat that covereth the inwards, and all the fat that is upon the inwards" — the rich fat, the choicest and best, given to God; the witnesses note the principle, that "the fat is the LORD's" (3:16), the best of the offering reserved for him; the witnesses note that in our fellowship with God the best and the inward is his; so that the fat, the best, is offered by fire to God; and all the fat that is upon the inwards. The witnesses note And he shall offer of the sacrifice of the peace offering an offering made by fire unto the LORD; the fat that covereth the inwards. The witnesses note and all the fat that is upon the inwards.

The witnesses note the fat that covered the inwards, the best and richest, offered by fire to the LORD, while the rest was shared — the fat being God's portion ("the fat is the LORD's"); some seeing the fat and the inward fat signify the inward affections and the best of the heart given to God, the inmost and the choicest devoted to him, in our fellowship with God the best and the inward his.

So the witnesses make the verse the giving of the fat, the best, to God. The lesson the expositors draw is that the fat, the choicest, was burnt to God while the rest was shared, the best and the inward given to him, our fellowship reserving the best for God. The witnesses note the the fat: "the fat that covereth the inwards" — Gill notes the richest; the the fat; the the richest; the the choicest; the the best; the to God. The expositors observe the not all: not all, but a part; the not all; the not all; the a part; the the rest shared; the the feast. The witnesses press the the fat the LORD's: "the fat is the LORD's" — reserved for him; the the fat the LORD's; the reserved for him; the the best; the his; the the offering. The expositors draw the lesson on the inward affections: the inward affections to God; the inward affections; the the inward; the affections; the the best of the heart; the to God. The expositors note the best is his: in fellowship the best and the inward is his. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and all the fat that is upon the inwards, and the fat. The verse thus records the giving of the fat — teaching that the best and richest was burnt to God, the inmost and choicest devoted to him.
4:
"And the two kidneys, and the fat that is on them, which is by the flanks, and the caul above the liver, with the kidneys, it shall he take away." The witnesses note the two kidneys, the fat on them, and the caul above the liver taken away for the offering. Gill notes the two kidneys next the flanks, with the fat on them, and the caul above the liver, were taken away to be burnt. The witnesses note God appointing the two kidneys, the fat on them, and the caul above the liver to be taken away and offered; the choice inward parts, with the fat, set apart for God — that the kidneys and the caul, with their fat, are God's: "the two kidneys, and the fat that is on them... and the caul above the liver, with the kidneys, it shall he take away"; the witnesses note the parts, "the two kidneys, and the fat that is on them," and "the caul above the liver" — the choice inner parts, with their rich fat, taken away from the rest; the witnesses note that these, with the fat (v.3), were the portion burnt to God, the best and inmost of the victim; the witnesses note the meaning some draw, that the kidneys (the reins, the seat of the inward affections and desires, "the reins" God tries, Ps. 7:9) and the caul above the liver, with the fat, signify the inmost affections and the richest of the heart, given to God; the witnesses note that God claims the inward parts, the secret affections and desires of the soul; the witnesses note that in true fellowship the heart and its desires are given to God; so that the kidneys and the caul, with the fat, are taken for God; it shall he take away. The witnesses note And the two kidneys, and the fat that is on them, which is by the flanks, and the caul above the liver, with the kidneys. The witnesses note it shall he take away.

The witnesses note the two kidneys, their fat, and the caul above the liver taken away and offered — the choice inner parts, with their rich fat, the best and inmost of the victim, given to God; some seeing the kidneys (the reins, the seat of the inward affections) and the caul signify the inmost affections and richest of the heart, God claiming the secret affections and desires of the soul.

So the witnesses make the verse the choice inward parts, with the fat, set apart for God. The lesson the expositors draw is that the kidneys and the caul, with their fat, were burnt to God, the inmost given to him, in true fellowship the heart and its desires given to God. The witnesses note the the kidneys: "the two kidneys, and the fat" — Gill notes by the flanks; the the kidneys; the by the flanks; the the fat; the on them; the the choice. The expositors observe the the caul: "the caul above the liver" — the inner parts; the the caul; the the inner parts; the with the kidneys; the the liver; the taken. The witnesses press the burnt to God: the portion burnt to God; the burnt to God; the the portion; the to God; the the best; the the inmost. The expositors draw the lesson on the the reins: the kidneys, the reins; the the reins; the the kidneys; the the seat; the the affections; the the desires. The expositors note the heart given: the heart and its desires given to God. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the it shall he take away, and the inward parts. The verse thus records the choice inward parts — teaching that the kidneys and caul, with the fat, were given to God, the inmost devoted to him.
5:
"And Aaron's sons shall burn it on the altar upon the burnt sacrifice, which is upon the wood that is on the fire: it is an offering made by fire, of a sweet savour unto the LORD." The witnesses note the fat burnt on the altar upon the burnt sacrifice, a sweet savour to the LORD. Gill notes the fat of the several parts was burnt on the altar upon the burnt offering, signifying that it was acceptable to God, an offering of a sweet savour. The witnesses note the priests burning the fat on the altar upon the burnt sacrifice, a sweet savour to the LORD; the burning of the fat upon the burnt offering, well-pleasing to God — that the peace offering's fat is burnt with the daily burnt offering, a sweet savour: "Aaron's sons shall burn it on the altar upon the burnt sacrifice... an offering made by fire, of a sweet savour unto the LORD"; the witnesses note the burning, "burn it on the altar upon the burnt sacrifice" — that the fat of the peace offering was laid and burnt "upon the burnt offering" (the continual sacrifice on the altar), the two going up together; the witnesses note the acceptance, "of a sweet savour unto the LORD" — that the fat, the best, burnt to God, was well-pleasing and accepted; the witnesses note the meaning the commentators draw, that the peace offering, the offering of fellowship, rested upon and was joined with the burnt offering of entire devotion, our communion with God grounded on and rising with the offering of Christ; the witnesses note that our peace and fellowship with God are accepted only as resting on the all-devoted sacrifice; the witnesses note that the best given to God in fellowship is a sweet savour; so that the fat is burnt upon the burnt offering, a sweet savour; of a sweet savour unto the LORD. The witnesses note And Aaron's sons shall burn it on the altar upon the burnt sacrifice, which is upon the wood that is on the fire. The witnesses note it is an offering made by fire, of a sweet savour unto the LORD.

The witnesses note the fat of the peace offering burnt on the altar upon the burnt sacrifice, the two going up together, a sweet savour to the LORD — the offering of fellowship resting upon and joined with the burnt offering of entire devotion; our communion with God grounded on and rising with the offering of Christ, our peace accepted only as resting on the all-devoted sacrifice.

So the witnesses make the verse the burning of the fat upon the burnt offering, well-pleasing to God. The lesson the expositors draw is that the peace offering's fat was burnt upon the burnt offering, the fellowship-offering joined with the offering of devotion, our peace resting on the all-devoted sacrifice. The witnesses note the burn upon the burnt sacrifice: "upon the burnt sacrifice" — Gill notes the two together; the burn upon the burnt sacrifice; the the two together; the the continual sacrifice; the laid; the burnt. The expositors observe the sweet savour: "of a sweet savour" — well-pleasing; the the best. The witnesses press the fellowship on devotion: fellowship rests on devotion; the the burnt offering. The expositors draw the lesson on the with the offering of Christ: rising with the offering of Christ. The expositors note the best a sweet savour: the best given to God a sweet savour. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the it is an offering made by fire of a sweet savour unto the LORD, and the fat. The verse thus records the burning of the fat — teaching that the peace offering's fat was burnt upon the burnt offering, our peace resting on the all-devoted sacrifice.
6:
"And if his offering for a sacrifice of peace offering unto the LORD be of the flock; male or female, he shall offer it without blemish." The witnesses note the peace offering of the flock, male or female, without blemish. Gill notes the peace offering might be of the flock, and be either male or female, but it must be without blemish. The witnesses note God appointing the peace offering of the flock to be male or female, without blemish; the lesser peace offering of the flock, the same in kind — that the flock-offering of peace, like the herd, may be of either sex, but unblemished: "if his offering for a sacrifice of peace offering... be of the flock; male or female, he shall offer it without blemish"; the witnesses note the kind, "of the flock" — a smaller offering than the herd, for those of less means; the witnesses note the latitude, "male or female" — that, as with the herd-offering, the peace offering of the flock might be of either sex, the greater freedom of the offering of communion; the witnesses note the requirement, "without blemish" — that, though either sex, it must be perfect and unblemished, a type of Christ the Lamb without blemish; the witnesses note that God made room for the peace offering of every estate, the same fellowship open to the poorer sort; the witnesses note the type before given, that the peace offering shadows Christ our peace, by whom all his people, rich or poor, have communion with God; the witnesses note that the same peace is offered in great and small; so that the flock peace offering is male or female, unblemished; he shall offer it without blemish. The witnesses note And if his offering for a sacrifice of peace offering unto the LORD be of the flock; male or female. The witnesses note he shall offer it without blemish.

The witnesses note the peace offering of the flock, male or female but unblemished, for those of less means — the same fellowship open to the poorer sort, God making room for the peace offering of every estate; a type of Christ our peace, by whom all his people, rich or poor, have communion with God, the same peace offered in great and small.

So the witnesses make the verse the lesser peace offering of the flock, the same in kind. The lesson the expositors draw is that the flock-offering of peace might be of either sex but unblemished, the same fellowship open to the poorer sort, the same Christ our peace. The witnesses note the of the flock: "of the flock" — Gill notes the smaller; the of the flock; the the smaller; the less means; the the poorer sort; the the offering. The expositors observe the male or female: "male or female" — the greater freedom; the male or female; the the greater freedom; the either sex; the communion; the as the herd. The witnesses press the without blemish: "without blemish" — perfect; the without blemish; the perfect; the the Lamb; the unblemished; the Christ. The expositors draw the lesson on the room for every estate: room for every estate; the room for every estate; the room; the every estate; the the poorer; the open. The expositors note the same peace: the same peace in great and small. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the he shall offer it without blemish, and the flock offering. The verse thus records the flock peace offering — teaching that it might be of either sex but unblemished, the same fellowship open to the poorer sort.
7:
"If he offer a lamb for his offering, then shall he offer it before the LORD." The witnesses note the lamb of the flock offered before the LORD. Gill notes if he offer a lamb, which was of the flock and must be of the first year; this is a rule, that wherever this word is used in the law, it signifies one of the first year. The witnesses note God appointing that, if a lamb be offered for the peace offering, it be brought before the LORD; the bringing of the lamb, of the first year, before God — that the lamb is offered in God's presence: "If he offer a lamb for his offering, then shall he offer it before the LORD"; the witnesses note the victim, "a lamb" — of the flock, "of the first year," young and perfect; the witnesses note the place, "before the LORD" — that it was brought into God's presence, at the appointed place, to be offered; the witnesses note the type the commentators draw, that the lamb, the young and unblemished victim, shadows Christ, "the Lamb of God" (John 1:29), the Lamb slain, offered before God for his people; the witnesses note that the lamb was ever a chief type of Christ, the meek and spotless victim; the witnesses note that the peace offering, of a lamb, points to the peace made by the Lamb of God; so that the lamb is offered before the LORD; then shall he offer it before the LORD. The witnesses note If he offer a lamb for his offering. The witnesses note then shall he offer it before the LORD.

The witnesses note the lamb of the first year offered for the peace offering before the LORD — young and perfect, brought into God's presence; a type of Christ, "the Lamb of God," the Lamb slain, offered before God for his people, the lamb ever a chief type of Christ, the meek and spotless victim, the peace offering of a lamb pointing to the peace made by the Lamb of God.

So the witnesses make the verse the bringing of the lamb before God. The lesson the expositors draw is that the lamb, of the first year, was offered before the LORD, a type of Christ the Lamb of God, offered before God for his people. The witnesses note the a lamb: "a lamb" — Gill notes of the first year; the a lamb; the of the first year; the young; the perfect; the the flock. The expositors observe the before the LORD: "before the LORD" — God's presence; the before the LORD; the God's presence; the the appointed place; the offered; the brought. The witnesses press the the Lamb of God: a type of the Lamb of God; the the Lamb of God; the the Lamb; the of God; the Christ; the slain. The expositors draw the lesson on the meek and spotless: the meek and spotless victim; the meek and spotless; the meek; the spotless; the the victim; the the lamb. The expositors note the peace by the Lamb: peace made by the Lamb of God. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the then shall he offer it before the LORD, and the lamb. The verse thus records the lamb offered — teaching that the lamb, of the first year, was offered before God, a type of Christ the Lamb of God.
8:
"And he shall lay his hand upon the head of his offering, and kill it before the tabernacle of the congregation: and Aaron's sons shall sprinkle the blood thereof round about upon the altar." The witnesses note the offerer laying his hand on the lamb, killing it, and the priests sprinkling its blood. Gill notes the Targum of Jonathan adds, his right hand with strength, on the head of the offering, the same as with the herd. The witnesses note the offerer laying his hand on the head of the lamb, killing it before the tabernacle, and the priests sprinkling its blood round the altar; the same laying on of hands and sprinkled blood for the lamb as for the herd — that the lamb too rests on the substitute and the blood: "he shall lay his hand upon the head of his offering, and kill it before the tabernacle... and Aaron's sons shall sprinkle the blood thereof round about upon the altar"; the witnesses note the laying on, "lay his hand upon the head" — the offerer identifying with the victim, the sin transferred, as before; the witnesses note the slaying, "kill it before the tabernacle of the congregation" — the lamb put to death in God's presence; the witnesses note the blood, "sprinkle the blood thereof round about upon the altar" — applied for atonement, as with the herd; the witnesses note that the same order and the same atoning ground held for the lamb, the peace and fellowship resting on the blood of the substitute; the witnesses note that there is no fellowship with God but through atonement; so that the lamb too rests on the substitute and the blood; sprinkle the blood thereof round about upon the altar. The witnesses note he shall lay his hand upon the head of his offering, and kill it before the tabernacle of the congregation. The witnesses note and Aaron's sons shall sprinkle the blood thereof round about upon the altar.

The witnesses note the offerer laying his hand on the lamb, killing it, and the priests sprinkling its blood — the same laying on of hands and sprinkled blood for the lamb as for the herd; the peace and fellowship resting on the blood of the substitute, our peace with God grounded on the death and blood of Christ the Lamb, no fellowship with God but through atonement.

So the witnesses make the verse the same laying on of hands and sprinkled blood for the lamb. The lesson the expositors draw is that the lamb too rested on the substitute and the blood, our peace grounded on the death of Christ the Lamb, no fellowship but through atonement. The witnesses note the lay his hand: "lay his hand upon the head" — Gill notes identifying; the lay his hand; the identifying; the the sin transferred; the the victim; the the head. The expositors observe the kill it: "kill it before the tabernacle" — in God's presence; the kill it; the in God's presence; the put to death; the the lamb; the slain. The witnesses press the sprinkle the blood: "sprinkle the blood... round about" — for atonement; the sprinkle the blood; the for atonement; the applied; the the altar; the as the herd. The expositors draw the lesson on the peace on the blood: peace rests on the blood; the peace on the blood; the peace; the rests; the on the blood; the the substitute. The expositors note the no fellowship but through atonement: no fellowship with God but through atonement. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and Aaron's sons shall sprinkle the blood thereof round about upon the altar, and the atonement. The verse thus records the hands and blood for the lamb — teaching that it too rested on the substitute and the blood, our peace grounded on the death of Christ.
9:
"And he shall offer of the sacrifice of the peace offering an offering made by fire unto the LORD; the fat thereof, and the whole rump, it shall he take off hard by the backbone; and the fat that covereth the inwards, and all the fat that is upon the inwards," The witnesses note the fat and the whole rump of the lamb offered to the LORD. Adam Clarke notes the whole rump, the fat tail, was taken off hard by the backbone; the eastern sheep have large, fat tails, sometimes weighing many pounds, accounted a great delicacy. The witnesses note God appointing the fat and the whole rump (the fat tail) of the lamb to be taken off and offered, with the fat covering the inwards; the giving of the fat and the rich fat-tail of the lamb to God — that the fat and the whole rump are God's: "the fat thereof, and the whole rump, it shall he take off hard by the backbone; and the fat that covereth the inwards, and all the fat that is upon the inwards"; the witnesses note the rump, "the whole rump... hard by the backbone" — the large fat tail of the eastern sheep, "accounted a great delicacy," the richest part of the lamb, here given to God; the witnesses note the fat, "the fat that covereth the inwards, and all the fat" — the inward fat also, as with the herd; the witnesses note that the best and richest of the lamb, the fat and the fat-tail, were burnt to God, the choicest reserved for him; the witnesses note the meaning before given, that the fat, the best, given to God, signifies the best and the inward devoted to him; the witnesses note that God claims the choicest in our fellowship; so that the fat and the whole rump are offered to God; and all the fat that is upon the inwards. The witnesses note the fat thereof, and the whole rump, it shall he take off hard by the backbone. The witnesses note and the fat that covereth the inwards, and all the fat that is upon the inwards.

The witnesses note the fat and the whole rump (the large fat tail of the eastern sheep, the richest part) of the lamb taken off and offered to the LORD, with the inward fat — the best and richest of the lamb burnt to God, the choicest reserved for him; the fat, the best, given to God signifying the best and the inward devoted to him, God claiming the choicest in our fellowship.

So the witnesses make the verse the giving of the fat and the rich fat-tail to God. The lesson the expositors draw is that the best and richest of the lamb, the fat and the fat-tail, were burnt to God, the choicest reserved for him, the best and the inward devoted. The witnesses note the the whole rump: "the whole rump... by the backbone" — Clarke notes the fat tail; the the whole rump; the the fat tail; the the eastern sheep; the a delicacy; the the richest. The expositors observe the the fat: "the fat that covereth the inwards" — the inward fat; the as the herd. The witnesses press the best to God: the best and richest to God; the burnt. The expositors draw the lesson on the choicest reserved: the choicest reserved for him. The expositors note the claims the choicest: God claims the choicest in our fellowship. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and the fat that covereth the inwards and all the fat that is upon the inwards, and the fat. The verse thus records the fat and the rump — teaching that the best and richest of the lamb was burnt to God, the choicest reserved for him.
10:
"And the two kidneys, and the fat that is upon them, which is by the flanks, and the caul above the liver, with the kidneys, it shall he take away." The witnesses note the two kidneys, their fat, and the caul above the liver of the lamb taken away. Charles Ellicott notes the same direction is given here as about the bullock of the peace offering, the kidneys, their fat, and the caul above the liver taken away. The witnesses note God appointing the two kidneys, the fat on them, and the caul above the liver of the lamb to be taken away and offered; the same choice inward parts of the lamb set apart for God, as of the herd — that the kidneys and the caul, with their fat, are God's: "the two kidneys, and the fat that is upon them... and the caul above the liver, with the kidneys, it shall he take away"; the witnesses note the parts, "the two kidneys... and the caul above the liver" — the same inner parts as in the herd-offering, with their rich fat; the witnesses note that "the same direction" was given for the lamb as for the bullock, the same portion taken for God; the witnesses note the meaning before given, that the kidneys (the reins, the seat of the inward affections) and the caul, with the fat, signify the inmost affections and the richest of the heart given to God; the witnesses note that God claims the inward and the best in the lamb as in the bullock, in the lesser offering as in the greater; the witnesses note that the heart and its desires are given to God; so that the kidneys and the caul of the lamb are taken for God; it shall he take away. The witnesses note And the two kidneys, and the fat that is upon them, which is by the flanks, and the caul above the liver, with the kidneys. The witnesses note it shall he take away.

The witnesses note the two kidneys, their fat, and the caul above the liver of the lamb taken away and offered, the same choice inward parts as of the herd — God claiming the inward and the best in the lamb as in the bullock; some seeing the kidneys (the reins, the seat of the inward affections) and the caul signify the inmost affections and richest of the heart given to God.

So the witnesses make the verse the same choice inward parts of the lamb set apart for God. The lesson the expositors draw is that the kidneys and the caul of the lamb, with their fat, were given to God, the same as the herd, the inward and best his in the lesser as in the greater. The witnesses note the the kidneys: "the two kidneys" — Ellicott notes the same direction; the the kidneys; the the same direction; the their fat; the the flanks; the the inner parts. The expositors observe the the caul: "the caul above the liver" — taken; the the caul; the taken; the with the kidneys; the the liver; the the same. The witnesses press the burnt to God: the portion to God; the burnt to God; the the portion; the to God; the the best; the the inmost. The expositors draw the lesson on the the reins: the kidneys, the reins; the the reins; the the kidneys; the the affections; the the desires; the the seat. The expositors note the in lesser and greater: his in the lamb as in the bullock. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the it shall he take away, and the inward parts. The verse thus records the inward parts of the lamb — teaching that the kidneys and caul were given to God, the inward and best his in the lesser as in the greater.
11:
"And the priest shall burn it upon the altar: it is the food of the offering made by fire unto the LORD." The witnesses note the priest burning the fat upon the altar, the food of the offering made by fire to the LORD. Gill notes the priest burnt the fat of the tail, of the inwards, the two kidneys, and the caul of the liver, called the food of the offering made by fire to the LORD. The witnesses note the priest burning the fat upon the altar, called the food of the offering made by fire to the LORD; the fat as God's "food," the portion of the offering that is his — that the fat is the bread or food of the offering: "the priest shall burn it upon the altar: it is the food of the offering made by fire unto the LORD"; the witnesses note the burning, "the priest shall burn it upon the altar" — that the fat, the rump, the kidneys, and the caul were all burnt to God; the witnesses note the striking phrase, "the food of the offering made by fire unto the LORD" — that the fat burnt to God is called his "food" or "bread," spoken after the manner of men, as the portion of the offering reserved for God, his table furnished from the altar; the witnesses note the meaning the commentators draw, that God speaks of the offering as his food, not as needing it (Ps. 50:12-13), but to mark it as his portion, given to him and accepted; the witnesses note the type, that Christ's offering is the bread of God's table, the satisfaction in which God is well-pleased, and that the sacrifice furnishes both God's portion and the believer's feast; the witnesses note that the best, burnt to God, is his food and delight; so that the fat is burnt, the food of the offering to God; it is the food of the offering made by fire unto the LORD. The witnesses note And the priest shall burn it upon the altar. The witnesses note it is the food of the offering made by fire unto the LORD.

The witnesses note the fat burnt upon the altar, called "the food of the offering made by fire unto the LORD" — the fat as God's "food" or "bread," spoken after the manner of men (not as needing it) to mark it as his portion, given to him and accepted; a type that Christ's offering is the bread of God's table, the satisfaction in which God is well-pleased, the sacrifice furnishing both God's portion and the believer's feast.

So the witnesses make the verse the fat as God's food, the portion of the offering that is his. The lesson the expositors draw is that the fat burnt to God is called his food or bread, marking it as his portion, a type of Christ's offering as the satisfaction in which God is well-pleased. The witnesses note the burn it: "burn it upon the altar" — Gill notes the fat and the rest; the the rump and kidneys. The expositors observe the the food: "the food of the offering" — God's bread; the his portion. The witnesses press the after the manner of men: spoken after the manner of men; the not needing it. The expositors draw the lesson on the Christ the bread: Christ the bread of God's table, the satisfaction. The expositors note the both portion and feast: God's portion and the believer's feast. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the it is the food of the offering made by fire unto the LORD, and the food. The verse thus records the fat as God's food — teaching that the fat burnt to God is called his food, marking it his portion, a type of Christ's offering in which God is well-pleased.
12:
"And if his offering be a goat, then he shall offer it before the LORD." The witnesses note the peace offering of a goat offered before the LORD. Adam Clarke notes "a goat," implying the whole species, he-goat, she-goat, and kid. The witnesses note God appointing that, if the peace offering be a goat, it be offered before the LORD; the goat of the flock for the peace offering, the third kind — that the goat too may be offered: "if his offering be a goat, then he shall offer it before the LORD"; the witnesses note the kind, "a goat" — of the flock, "implying the whole species, he-goat, she-goat, and kid," another sort of peace offering; the witnesses note the place, "before the LORD" — that it was brought into God's presence, at the appointed place; the witnesses note that the peace offering might be of the herd (bullock), or of the flock (lamb or goat), God making room for various offerings according to men's substance; the witnesses note the type before given, that all these clean victims shadow Christ, the one true peace offering, of whom all spoke; the witnesses note that the same fellowship and peace were open through every kind of offering; so that the goat too is offered before the LORD; then he shall offer it before the LORD. The witnesses note And if his offering be a goat. The witnesses note then he shall offer it before the LORD.

The witnesses note the peace offering of a goat (the whole species, he-goat, she-goat, and kid) offered before the LORD — the third kind of victim; the peace offering might be of the herd or flock, God making room for various offerings according to men's substance; all these clean victims shadowing Christ, the one true peace offering, the same fellowship and peace open through every kind.

So the witnesses make the verse the goat of the flock for the peace offering. The lesson the expositors draw is that the goat too might be offered before the LORD, God making room for various offerings, all shadowing Christ the one true peace offering. The witnesses note the a goat: "a goat" — Clarke notes the whole species; the a goat; the the whole species; the he-goat; the she-goat; the kid. The expositors observe the before the LORD: "before the LORD" — God's presence; the before the LORD; the God's presence; the the appointed place; the offered; the brought. The witnesses press the herd or flock: of the herd or flock; the herd or flock; the the herd; the the flock; the various; the the substance. The expositors draw the lesson on the shadow Christ: all shadow Christ; the shadow Christ; the all; the shadow; the Christ; the the one. The witnesses observe the one peace offering: the one true peace offering; the one peace offering; the the one; the true; the peace offering; the of whom all spoke. The expositors note the same fellowship: the same fellowship through every kind; the same fellowship; the the same; the fellowship; the every kind; the open. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the And if his offering be a goat, the then he shall offer it before the LORD, and the goat. The verse thus records the goat offering — teaching that the goat too might be offered, God making room for various offerings, all shadowing Christ.
13:
"And he shall lay his hand upon the head of it, and kill it before the tabernacle of the congregation: and the sons of Aaron shall sprinkle the blood thereof upon the altar round about." The witnesses note the offerer laying his hand on the goat, killing it, and the priests sprinkling its blood. Gill notes the same directions are given for the killing of it and the sprinkling of its blood as in the offerings of the bullock and lamb. The witnesses note the offerer laying his hand on the head of the goat, killing it before the tabernacle, and the priests sprinkling its blood round the altar; the same laying on of hands and sprinkled blood for the goat as for the rest — that the goat too rests on the substitute and the blood: "he shall lay his hand upon the head of it, and kill it before the tabernacle... and the sons of Aaron shall sprinkle the blood thereof upon the altar round about"; the witnesses note the laying on, "lay his hand upon the head" — the offerer identifying with the victim, the sin transferred, as before; the witnesses note the slaying and the blood, "kill it... and... sprinkle the blood... round about" — the "same directions... as in the offerings of the bullock and lamb," the victim slain and its blood applied for atonement; the witnesses note that the same atoning ground held for every kind of peace offering, the fellowship resting on the blood of the substitute; the witnesses note that there is one way of peace, by the blood of the substitute, in every offering; so that the goat too rests on the substitute and the blood; sprinkle the blood thereof upon the altar round about. The witnesses note And he shall lay his hand upon the head of it, and kill it before the tabernacle of the congregation. The witnesses note and the sons of Aaron shall sprinkle the blood thereof upon the altar round about.

The witnesses note the offerer laying his hand on the goat, killing it, and the priests sprinkling its blood — the same laying on of hands and sprinkled blood for the goat as for the bullock and lamb; the same atoning ground for every kind of peace offering, the fellowship resting on the blood of the substitute, one way of peace by the blood, our peace grounded on the death and blood of Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse the same laying on of hands and sprinkled blood for the goat. The lesson the expositors draw is that the goat too rested on the substitute and the blood, the same atoning ground for every kind, one way of peace by the blood. The witnesses note the lay his hand: "lay his hand upon the head" — Gill notes identifying; the lay his hand; the identifying; the the sin transferred; the the victim; the the head. The expositors observe the kill it: "kill it before the tabernacle" — the same directions; the kill it; the the same directions; the as the bullock; the and lamb; the slain. The witnesses press the sprinkle the blood: "sprinkle the blood... round about" — for atonement; the sprinkle the blood; the for atonement; the applied; the the altar; the as the rest. The expositors draw the lesson on the same ground: the same atoning ground; the same ground; the the same; the atoning; the ground; the every kind. The expositors note the death of Christ: grounded on the death of Christ. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and the sons of Aaron shall sprinkle the blood thereof upon the altar round about, and the atonement. The verse thus records the hands and blood for the goat — teaching that it too rested on the substitute and the blood, one way of peace by the blood.
14:
"And he shall offer thereof his offering, even an offering made by fire unto the LORD; the fat that covereth the inwards, and all the fat that is upon the inwards," The witnesses note the fat that covered the inwards of the goat offered by fire to the LORD. Gill notes the same rules are laid down about taking the fat off the several parts as in the sacrifice of the bullock; but nothing is said of the fat of the rump and tail, as is said of the lamb. The witnesses note God appointing the fat that covered the inwards and all the fat upon them of the goat to be offered by fire; the giving of the fat of the goat to God, as of the rest, though without the fat-tail — that the fat of the goat too is God's: "he shall offer thereof his offering... the fat that covereth the inwards, and all the fat that is upon the inwards"; the witnesses note the parts, "the fat that covereth the inwards, and all the fat" — the same inward fat as in the bullock; the witnesses note the difference noted, that "nothing is said of the fat of the rump and tail," as with the lamb, the goat having no such fat-tail; the witnesses note that, save for that, "the same rules" held, the fat and the inward parts given to God; the witnesses note the meaning before given, that the fat, the best, given to God, signifies the best and the inward devoted to him; the witnesses note that God claims the choicest in every kind of offering; so that the fat of the goat too is offered to God; and all the fat that is upon the inwards. The witnesses note And he shall offer thereof his offering, even an offering made by fire unto the LORD; the fat that covereth the inwards. The witnesses note and all the fat that is upon the inwards.

The witnesses note the fat that covered the inwards of the goat offered by fire to the LORD, the same as the bullock (though the goat had no fat-tail like the lamb) — the fat, the best, given to God, signifying the best and the inward devoted to him; God claiming the choicest in every kind of offering.

So the witnesses make the verse the giving of the fat of the goat to God. The lesson the expositors draw is that the fat and inward parts of the goat were given to God, the same as the bullock, the best and the inward devoted, God claiming the choicest in every kind. The witnesses note the the fat: "the fat that covereth the inwards" — Gill notes the same rules; the the fat; the the same rules; the the inward fat; the as the bullock; the all the fat. The expositors observe the no rump: "nothing... of the rump and tail" — the goat had none; the no rump; the the goat had none; the unlike the lamb; the no fat-tail; the the difference. The witnesses press the best to God: the fat, the best, to God; the best to God; the the fat; the the best; the to God; the burnt. The expositors draw the lesson on the inward devoted: the best and the inward devoted; the inward devoted; the the best; the the inward; the devoted; the to him. The expositors note the God claims: God claims the choicest in every kind. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and all the fat that is upon the inwards, and the fat. The verse thus records the fat of the goat — teaching that it too was given to God, the best and the inward devoted, God claiming the choicest in every kind.
16:
"And the priest shall burn them upon the altar: it is the food of the offering made by fire for a sweet savour: all the fat is the LORD's." The witnesses note the priest burning the fat and inward parts on the altar, the food of the offering, a sweet savour; all the fat is the LORD's. Gill notes this shows that not the fat only, but the inwards and the kidneys, were burnt also; the priest salted the parts and burned them on the altar, for all the fat is the LORD's. The witnesses note the priest burning the fat and the inward parts on the altar, the food of the offering made by fire for a sweet savour, for all the fat is the LORD's; the burning of the fat as God's portion and the great rule, all the fat is his — that all the fat belongs to God: "the priest shall burn them upon the altar: it is the food of the offering made by fire for a sweet savour: all the fat is the LORD's"; the witnesses note the burning, "burn them upon the altar" — that "not the fat only, but the inwards and the kidneys" were burnt, salted and laid on the altar; the witnesses note the food, "the food of the offering... for a sweet savour" — the fat as God's portion, well-pleasing and accepted; the witnesses note the great rule, "all the fat is the LORD's" — that the fat, the best and richest, in every offering, belonged to God, reserved wholly for him; the witnesses note that the best is to be given to God, not kept for ourselves; the witnesses note that what is wholly God's is a sweet savour; so that all the fat is burnt to God, his portion; all the fat is the LORD's. The witnesses note And the priest shall burn them upon the altar: it is the food of the offering made by fire for a sweet savour. The witnesses note all the fat is the LORD's.

The witnesses note the priest burning the fat and inward parts on the altar, the food of the offering, a sweet savour — "all the fat is the LORD's," the great rule that the fat, the best and richest in every offering, belonged wholly to God; God claiming the best of all, the choicest of our substance and the inmost of our hearts, the best to be given to him, not kept for ourselves.

So the witnesses make the verse the burning of the fat as God's portion and the rule, all the fat is his. The lesson the expositors draw is that the fat, the best, in every offering belonged wholly to God, who claims the choicest of our substance and the inmost of our hearts, the best given to him. The witnesses note the burn them: "burn them upon the altar" — Gill notes the fat and the inwards; the the kidneys; the salted. The expositors observe the the food: "the food of the offering... a sweet savour" — God's portion; the accepted. The witnesses press the all the fat is the LORD's: "all the fat is the LORD's" — the great rule; the reserved wholly his. The expositors draw the lesson on the the best of all: God claims the best of all; the the best of all; the God claims; the the best; the of all; the the choicest. The expositors note the not kept for ourselves: the best given to God, not kept. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the all the fat is the LORD's, and the fat. The verse thus records the rule that all the fat is the LORD's — teaching that the best, in every offering, belonged wholly to God, who claims the choicest of our substance and the inmost of our hearts.
17:
"It shall be a perpetual statute for your generations throughout all your dwellings, that ye eat neither fat nor blood." The witnesses note the perpetual statute that they should eat neither fat nor blood. Gill notes it was a perpetual statute, unto the end of the Mosaic dispensation, until the Messiah comes and his sacrifice is offered and his blood is shed; throughout all their dwellings, that they eat neither fat nor blood. The witnesses note God making it a perpetual statute, in all their generations and dwellings, that they eat neither fat nor blood; the lasting law reserving the fat and the blood from common use — that fat and blood are God's, not man's food: "a perpetual statute for your generations throughout all your dwellings, that ye eat neither fat nor blood"; the witnesses note the perpetuity, "a perpetual statute for your generations" — binding "unto the end of the Mosaic dispensation, until the Messiah comes... and his blood is shed," in all generations and "throughout all your dwellings"; the witnesses note the fat, that the fat was forbidden as food because it was the LORD's portion (v.16), reserved for God; the witnesses note the blood, that the blood was forbidden because "the life of the flesh is in the blood," and the blood was given to make atonement (Lev. 17:11), too sacred for common use; the witnesses note the great meaning the commentators draw, that the fat (the best) and the blood (the life and the means of atonement) were kept sacred to God, the blood pointing to the atoning blood of Christ, not to be profaned; the witnesses note that the law taught a holy reverence for the blood, the price of atonement; the witnesses note that the things of God are to be held sacred; so that fat and blood are reserved from common use, God's alone; that ye eat neither fat nor blood. The witnesses note It shall be a perpetual statute for your generations throughout all your dwellings. The witnesses note that ye eat neither fat nor blood.

The witnesses note the perpetual statute that they eat neither fat nor blood — the fat forbidden as the LORD's portion, the blood because "the life of the flesh is in the blood" and the blood was given to make atonement (Leviticus 17:11), too sacred for common use; the blood pointing to the atoning blood of Christ, not to be profaned, the law teaching a holy reverence for the blood, the price of atonement.

So the witnesses make the verse the lasting law reserving fat and blood from common use. The lesson the expositors draw is that the fat (the LORD's portion) and the blood (the life and means of atonement) were kept sacred to God, the blood pointing to the atoning blood of Christ. The witnesses note the perpetual statute: "a perpetual statute" — Gill notes till Messiah; the perpetual statute; the till Messiah; the his blood shed; the all generations; the all dwellings. The expositors observe the the fat: the fat the LORD's portion; the the fat; the the LORD's portion; the reserved; the for God; the forbidden. The witnesses press the the blood: the blood, the life; the the blood; the the life; the of the flesh; the makes atonement; the sacred. The expositors draw the lesson on the atoning blood of Christ: pointing to the atoning blood of Christ; the atoning blood of Christ; the pointing; the the atoning blood; the of Christ; the not profaned. The expositors note the held sacred: the things of God held sacred. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the that ye eat neither fat nor blood, and the statute. The verse thus records the lasting law — teaching that fat and blood were reserved from common use, sacred to God, the blood pointing to the atoning blood of Christ.
